request.question: Which artist did Anthony Toby "Tony" Hiller appear with that liked showering himself (and others) with confetti?

title: Ichthyosis with confetti

sentences: Ichthyosis en confetti, also known as ichthyosis with confetti, congenital reticular ichthyosiform erythroderma (CRIE) and ichthyosis variegata, is a very rare form of congenital ichthyosis in which healthy patches of normal skin co-exist within the abnormal skin areas.

The condition is caused by a frameshift mutation in the keratin 10 gene (KRT10); mutant keratin 10 accumulates in the nucleolus, a sub-nuclear structure, rather than within cellular intermedite filaments like the wild-type protein.
Children with the condition exhibit red, flaky skin; however, for reasons not yet totally clear, wild type clonal patches of skin start to appear, in place of the red, flaky skin.
Due to the clonal nature of the growth of the normal skin cells, it appears the patient is covered with confetti, hence the name of the condition.
It has been hypothesized that this is the result of a combination of mitotic recombination and natural selection within the skin.
Anthony Toby "Tony" Hiller (born 30 July 1927) is a British songwriter.
He began his musical career as a member of the song and dance duo The Hiller Brothers, sharing the stage with his brother Irving.

The Hiller Brothers appeared with many artists of the time including Alma Cogan, Tommy Cooper, Val Doonican, Matt Monro, The Shadows, Bernard Manning, Kathy Kirby, Roger Whittaker, Rip Taylor, Gene Vincent, Lance Percival, Tessie O'Shea, Frank Ifield, Deep River Boys, The Dallas Boys, Clark Brothers, Paul Melba and Ray Burns.
Kevin Nicholas Drew, professionally and better known as KDrew, is an artist, producer and DJ from New Jersey.
KDrew produces primarily EDM and is perhaps best known for his remixes for Zedd and Lady Gaga, although his original records "Bullseye" and Circles have generated millions of plays online.
The Boston Celtics of the NBA currently use "Circles" as the intro music for their home games at TD Garden.
KDrew has also done official remixes for Nelly Furtado, Adventure Club, T.I. and others and collaborated with Taryn Manning on "Summer Ashes", which was released on July 23, 2013.
On February 5, 2015 MTV premiered KDrew's music video for "Let Me Go".
KDrew performed at Global Dance Festival at Red Rocks Amphitheatre.
He co-wrote the song "I Want You to Know", by Zedd featuring Selena Gomez, alongside Zedd and Ryan Tedder.
KDrew also features as an additional producer on the tracks "Done With Love" and "Straight Into the Fire" off Zedd's "True Colors" album.
KDrew co-produced two songs on Hilary Duff's Breathe In.
Breathe Out.
album, "Confetti" and "Arms Around a Memory".
Residential water use (also called domestic use, household use, or tap water use) includes all indoor and outdoor uses of drinking quality water at single-family and multifamily dwellings.

These uses include a number of defined purposes (or water end uses) such as flushing toilets, washing clothes and dishes, showering and bathing, drinking, food preparation, watering lawns and gardens, and maintaining swimming pools.
Some of these end uses are detectable (and measurable) while others are more difficult to gauge.
This list of most disliked YouTube videos contains the top 40 videos with the most dislikes of all time, as derived from YouTube charts.
The dislike count is taken directly from the page of the video itself.
YouTube implemented a like and dislike button on these pages in 2010, part of a major redesign of the site.

This served as a replacement for their five-star rating system; YouTube's designers found the previous system ineffective because the options to rate a video between two and four stars were rarely selected.
Of the 40 videos in this list, 14 also appear in the list of most viewed YouTube videos and 10 appear in the list of most liked YouTube videos.
Coleonema pulchellum, commonly known as confetti bush, buchu, diosma or breath of heaven, is a shrub which is endemic to South Africa.
It grows to between 0.8 and 1 metre high.
Single pink flowers to about 8 mm in diameter appear between May and October in the species native range.
Toby Love is the self-titled debut studio album by Puerto Rican-American performer Toby Love.
It was released on September 5, 2006 by Sony BMG Norte.
The album featured collaborations with Puerto Rican rapper Voltio and Puerto Rican duo R.K.M & Ken-Y, among others.

Upon release, the album debuted at number twenty-seven on the "Billboard" Latin Albums chart, number forty-two on the "Billboard" Heatseekers Albums chart, number three on the Mid-Atlantic area division of the "Billboard" Heatseekers Albums chart and number eleven on "Billboard" Latin Rhythm Albums chart.

The album spawned three singles, with the lead single, "Tengo Un Amor", becoming a success, in the Latin market, reaching number one in the "Billboard" Latin Rhythm Airplay chart, the top three of both the "Billboard" Latin Songs and "Billboard" Tropical Songs charts, while peaking at number 100 on the "Billboard" Hot 100 chart in the United States.
"Toby Love" was awarded a "Billboard" Latin Music Award for "R&B/Hip-Hop Album of the Year".
This list of most liked YouTube videos contains the top 40 videos with the most likes of all time, as derived from YouTube charts.
The like count is taken directly from the page of the video itself.
YouTube implemented a like and dislike button on these pages in March 2010, part of a major redesign of the site.

This served as a replacement for their five-star rating system; YouTube's designers found the previous system ineffective because the options to rate a video between two and four stars were rarely selected.
Of the 40 videos in this list, 37 also appear in the list of most viewed YouTube videos and 15 appear in the list of most disliked YouTube videos.
All videos in this list are music videos.

"There's No Place Like London" is a song co-written by Lynsey de Paul and Gerard Kenny, that was first recorded by Shirley Bassey with a 54 piece orchestra, produced by de Paul and released as a single in 1986.

She performed it in 1986 as part of her “Live from the Piccadilly” show as well as on the 1987 Royal Variety Performance and ironically for her "Shirley Bassey live" in Berlin in honour of 750 years of Berlin in 1987.
It was released for the first time on CD to tie-in with the UK held Olympic Games in 2012 on the album, "Olympic Tracks".
It is listed in the BBC music database which catalogues songs they play.
Speaking about the recording of the song in an interview, de Paul said "we wrote.
"There's No Place Like London", and the Tourist Board liked it, and then we thought of an artist and I thought of Shirley Bassey.
So I approached her and asked her to sing it and she really liked and that's the way round it came, it didn't come that I wrote for her I just had the song and said would you sing it.
And she was great."
It is still one of the most played Bassey songs on BBC radio.
Two different live Bassey performances of the song appear on the Odeon Entertainment 2010 DVD, "Shirley Bassey On TV".
Charles Elmer "Rip" Taylor, Jr. (born January 13, 1935) is an American actor and comedian.
He is known for his exuberance and flamboyant personality, including his wild moustache and his habit of showering himself (and others) with confetti.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- If the `passages` consistently corroborate the context claim and are identity-aligned, append one brief paraphrased supporting sentence that cites the corroboration (keep the summary compact).
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: Which artist did Anthony Toby "Tony" Hiller appear with that liked showering himself (and others) with confetti?
hop1_docs: Tony Hiller | Anthony Toby "Tony" Hiller (born 30 July 1927) is a British songwriter.  He began his musical career as a member of the song and dance duo The Hiller Brothers, sharing the stage with his brother Irving.  The Hiller Brothers appeared with many artists of the time including Alma Cogan, Tommy Cooper, Val Doonican, Matt Monro, The Shadows, Bernard Manning, Kathy Kirby, Roger Whittaker, Rip Taylor, Gene Vincent, Lance Percival, Tessie O'Shea, Frank Ifield, Deep River Boys, The Dallas Boys, Clark Brothers, Paul Melba and Ray Burns.

Rip Taylor | Charles Elmer "Rip" Taylor, Jr. (born January 13, 1935) is an American actor and comedian.  He is known for his exuberance and flamboyant personality, including his wild moustache and his habit of showering himself (and others) with confetti.

Ichthyosis with confetti | Ichthyosis en confetti, also known as ichthyosis with confetti, congenital reticular ichthyosiform erythroderma (CRIE) and ichthyosis variegata, is a very rare form of congenital ichthyosis in which healthy patches of normal skin co-exist within the abnormal skin areas.  The condition is caused by a frameshift mutation in the keratin 10 gene (KRT10); mutant keratin 10 accumulates in the nucleolus, a sub-nuclear structure, rather than within cellular intermedite filaments like the wild-type protein.  Children with the condition exhibit red, flaky skin; however, for reasons not yet totally clear, wild type clonal patches of skin start to appear, in place of the red, flaky skin.  Due to the clonal nature of the growth of the normal skin cells, it appears the patient is covered with confetti, hence the name of the condition.  It has been hypothesized that this is the result of a combination of mitotic recombination and natural selection within the skin.

Tony Booth (artist) | Anthony "Tony" Booth (22 June 1933 – 11 January 2017) was a British commercial artist best known as the original poster artist for The Beatles and other bands in the Merseybeat era.  He was known around Liverpool in the early 1960s as Brian Epstein's "right-hand man".

Scurfield | The family name Scurfield appears to be of Old Norse, or Viking origin.  “Scur” in Old English is “shower” as in rain.  In Old Norse “skur” also carries the meaning shower, as in shower of rain, or a shower of arrows.  The most likely origin of the surname Scurfield, therefore, is the name of an ancient field of battle.

Tony Reali | Anthony Joseph Paul "Tony" Reali (born July 4, 1978) is an American sports personality and television host of "Around the Horn" on ESPN.  In April 2014 it was announced he would be a contributor on social media for "Good Morning America", which he began on October 8, 2014 but has not appeared in 2015, other than a couple sports related segments introduced as "ESPN's Tony Reali".  He was "Stat Boy" on "Pardon the Interruption" from the show's debut in 2001 until late 2014.

The Careless Years | The Careless Years is a 1957 film from United Artists directed by Arthur Hiller and produced by Edward Lewis.  The film was the directorial debut for Hiller.  The film stars Dean Stockwell and Natalie Trundy in an early film appearance.

Bridge entity: Rip Taylor — next hop can verify the performance/appearance link between Tony Hiller (The Hiller Brothers) and Rip Taylor in independent sources if needed.

The Hiller Brothers appeared with many artists of the time including Alma Cogan, Tommy Cooper, Val Doonican, Matt Monro, The Shadows, Bernard Manning, Kathy Kirby, Roger Whittaker, Rip Taylor, Gene Vincent, Lance Percival, Tessie O'Shea, Frank Ifield, Deep River Boys, The Dallas Boys, Clark Brothers, Paul Melba and Ray Burns.

Bernard Hiller | Bernard Hiller, aka "Bernardo" born April 18, 1962 is an Actor, Singer, Dancer, Hollywood acting teacher, Producer, Writer, and Success coach.  Hiller was a Broadway performer in many musicals including "West Side Story" and Fiddler on the Roof".  He appeared in several TV shows and films such as Jean Paul in Raising Helen, and Max Lansky in Lansky.  He also appeared in over 200 TV commercials.
Hiller Aircraft | Hiller Aircraft Company was founded in 1942 as Hiller Industries by Stanley Hiller to develop helicopters.
Hiller Ten99 | The Hiller Ten99 (also known as the Hiller 1099) was an American 1961 experimental helicopter, created by Hiller Aircraft.
Max Hiller | Max Hiller (?  - 18 December 1948) was a German actor.  He appeared in more than twenty films from 1917 to 1948.
Lejaren Hiller, Sr. | Lejaren Hiller, Sr. / Lejaren à Hiller / John Hiller (3 July 1880 – 23 May 1969) was an accomplished American illustrator and photographer.

Hiller Aviation Museum | The Hiller Aviation Museum is an aircraft history museum located at the San Carlos Airport in San Carlos, California.  The museum was founded by Stanley Hiller in June 1998.  and is endowed by members of the Hiller family, owners of the Hiller Aircraft Corporation.  It specializes in Northern California aircraft history and helicopter history.  The museum is also an affiliate within the Smithsonian Affiliations program.
